## [00:00 - 03:00]

What's up everyone? I am Jeremy Broner. I'm the sales director here at Mastermind. My pleasure today. We're going to talk about the sales conversation or some people call this sales script. So there's five points that we're going to go over today and each of those points have three bullet points underneath them. Now we did that so you can follow along. So we're going to jump into number one, which is connection. So this is the very beginning of your sales call and obviously you don't want to jump right in and say, hey, here's my program, here's what offers, blah, blah, no, no, no, we want to build rapport with your prospect. And I've always asked this question to people like, hey, what's rapport? And I never get a clear answer. Everyone's like, what's this? What's that? What I want to do is I'm going to give you the definition so you truly understand what connection and rapport is. So here it is. You find out about the prospect. The prospect finds out about you and in the middle you find something to connect on. You find something that's a common ground. Now when I see this, the problem for most people is it's one-sided. Hey, you find out about the prospect, great. And they jump into the sales call. Or it's the other one where it's the me show, I've done this, I've done that, here's my program and nothing about them. And for this to work, there has to be a connection in the middle. So the three bullet points in this section I want you to write down F, F, and yes, the third one will be F. So the first F is going to be family. You're going to find out about their family, if they're married, if they got kids. The second F is going to be where they're from. The third F is what they do for fun. And you're going to ask these to connect with them. So here's the framework of what we'll do and here's what it sounds like. Client, before we get started, do you mind if I learn a little bit more about whom speaking to? But before we do that, I'd like to give you a little background about me, if that's okay. They say yes. And then I'm going to go through mine. So obviously, you know I'm Jeremy, I'm actually originally from Pennsylvania. I moved to Arizona 16 years ago. I met my wife in the gym 10 years ago and we're busy. We're always taking care of our, we got two kids and anytime that we're not doing that, my hobby is I love the DJ. I love music. Any event, anything I can DJ to, I'll do it. And I have over 200 playlist online. So tell me, tell me a little about yourself. Now since you started that conversation up, they feel they know you a little bit more. They're going to find a connection. Maybe it's the kids, maybe it's Pennsylvania, maybe it's the music. Oh wait, you like music? What's the music you like? Who's your favorite DJ? What concerts you've been to for a while? And that is how you talk to them.

## [12:05 - 15:06]

Number one, why now? Number two, why not six months ago? Number three, what does six months from now look like? And number four, last time I tossed in there, what's your end goal here? Now, as you use this, you're going to start to come up with your own questions that pertain to your offer. But you can see this is a good starting point to really just ask them why. And here's the thing, once you paint that picture of them going from Pain Island to Pleasure or the extra island, your job's done. They're thinking differently about why they're on this call versus, oh yeah, I'm good. So that is the discovery. And guys, it's super important on that discovery. We don't skip over it. So now we're going to move on to number three, which is the offer. Okay? This is where you're going to give an overview of your offer and how it can help them and what your place point is. This one's a little bit shorter overall for the sales conversation, but definitely don't overlook this one. And I want to start off by asking a silly question. If you saw a pedestrian get hit by a car and the car drove away, what would you do? You wouldn't think twice, right? It's your obligation. You'd run over to them and you would say, hey, hey, I can help. I can call an ambulance. I can wrap your wound. I can elevate your head. What do you need help with? In the sales conversation, the offer is exactly the same. Remember, they're in a little bit of pain, okay? So you're saying, I can help. My program offers you this. It offers you this and this. What do you need help with? Now guys, remember sales is a service. They're in pain. They need your services. So this is where you can offer that to them and help them out. Here's going to be the framework I'm going to give you to have your offer. So it starts off like this. So based on what you told me so far, I know I can help you get you from where you're currently at to where you want to get to. Hey, would you like to hear my thoughts? Now what that does, that's ask for permission. And then after they say, yes, of course, here's where we're going to go through. Pillar one, pillar two, and pillar three about your program. So pillar one, I want to make it clear of what my offer is. Pillar two, I want to make it proprietary. I want to make it sound different than what else is out there. And pillar three is going to be powerful. You don't want to make it sound like you're giving away a stick of gum. You want to make it sound powerful. Now, keep it simple. These pillars are just parts or phases of your program broken down. So next to the bullet points for this section, I want you to write down pillar one, pillar two, and yes, pillar three.

## [15:06 - 18:08]

Make it clear, make it proprietary, and make it powerful. So I'm going to give you an example of an offer that I'm going to make up just so we can use today.
